title: Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 4

patient.name: William Brown
patient.persona: retired factory worker, diabetic and hypertensive, missed multiple follow-up appointments

chief_complaint: I've been feeling more tired than usual. My legs are swollen and I can't fit into my shoes. I don't pee as much as I used to. My doctor said something about my 'kidney numbers' being bad.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Regular rate, S4 gallop, no murmurs or rubs
physical_exam.Abdomen: No bruits, no masses, no CVA tenderness
physical_exam.Extremities: Bilateral pitting edema to knees. No asterixis. No pericardial rub.
physical_exam.Skin: Dry, scaly. No rash. Uremic frost absent.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Alert, oriented. No asterixis. Mild cognitive slowing.

hidden_data.cmp: Sodium 138, Potassium 5.6, Chloride 105, HCO3 18, BUN 65, Creatinine 4.2, Glucose 180, Calcium 8.2, Phosphorus 5.8
hidden_data.eGFR: eGFR 16 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD Stage 4 - severely decreased kidney function)
hidden_data.cbc: WBC 6,500, Hb 9.2 (anemia of CKD - normocytic), Hct 28%, Platelets 175,000
hidden_data.urinalysis: Proteinuria 3+ (nephrotic range), No hematuria, No casts, Specific gravity 1.010 (isosthenuria - fixed)
hidden_data.renal_us: Bilateral small, echogenic kidneys (Right 8.2cm, Left 8.5cm). Cortical thinning. Loss of corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No stones.

correct_diagnosis: Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 4 due to Diabetic Nephropathy and Hypertensive Nephrosclerosis, with Secondary Hyperparathyroidism and Anemia of CKD
differential_diagnoses: Acute Kidney Injury superimposed on CKD
key_learning_points: CKD = kidney damage or GFR <60 mL/min for ≥3 months. Staged by GFR: Stage 1 (≥90), Stage 2 (60-89), Stage 3 (30-59), Stage 4 (15-29), Stage 5 (<15 = ESRD)
Most common causes: Diabetes (40%), Hypertension (30%), Glomerulonephritis, Polycystic kidney disease
Complications of CKD: Anemia (decreased EPO), CKD-MBD (phosphate retention → secondary hyperparathyroidism), Hyperkalemia, Metabolic acidosis, Volume overload
Small, echogenic kidneys on ultrasound = chronic irreversible disease (not acute)
Management: BP control (target <130/80), ACEi/ARB for proteinuria, glycemic control (HbA1c <7%), treat complications, nephrology referral for CKD Stage 4

patient_responses.history: I've had diabetes for 20 years and high blood pressure for 15. I admit I don't check my sugar much. I take my meds but I've missed my last 3 appointments.
patient_responses.symptoms: I'm tired all the time. I get short of breath walking to the mailbox. My legs swell by evening. I don't have much appetite. Food tastes metallic.
patient_responses.urine: I used to pee a lot, especially at night. Now I don't go as much. It's foamy - my wife noticed bubbles in the toilet.
patient_responses.medications: Metformin 1000mg BID, Glipizide 10mg BID, Lisinopril 20mg, Amlodipine 10mg, Furosemide 40mg BID. I take ibuprofen for my knee pain almost daily.

hints.history: Long-standing DM and HTN are the most common causes of CKD. Ask about NSAID use (nephrotoxic!). Symptoms of uremia: fatigue, anorexia, metallic taste, pruritus.
hints.examination: Look for volume overload (edema, crackles, JVD), anemia (pallor), uremia (asterixis, pericardial rub, encephalopathy), and CKD-MBD (bone pain).
hints.labs: CMP (elevated BUN/Cr, hyperkalemia, low HCO3, high phosphorus, low calcium). CBC (anemia). Urinalysis (proteinuria, hematuria, casts). HbA1c. PTH, vitamin D.
hints.imaging: Renal ultrasound: Small kidneys = chronic disease. Normal/large kidneys with AKI suggests acute or acute-on-chronic. Rule out obstruction.

feedback.correct_message: Well done! You correctly diagnosed Stage 4 CKD from the markedly elevated creatinine (4.2), small echogenic kidneys on US, nephrotic-range proteinuria, and complications (anemia, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, secondary hyperparathyroidism). The long-standing DM and HTN with poor control are the underlying causes. Nephrology referral is appropriate.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good recognition of renal failure! However, the small echogenic kidneys on ultrasound and chronic symptoms indicate this is CKD Stage 4, not AKI. The complications (anemia, CKD-MBD, acidosis) are typical of chronic disease. AKI would show normal/large kidneys and lack anemia/hyperparathyroidism.
feedback.incorrect_message: This is Stage 4 CKD (eGFR 16) from diabetic nephropathy and hypertensive nephrosclerosis. The small echogenic kidneys, nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR 4.2), anemia (Hb 9.2), and secondary hyperparathyroidism (PTH 280) confirm chronic irreversible disease. STOP metformin (contraindicated when eGFR <30) and ibuprofen (nephrotoxic). Refer to nephrology for renal replacement therapy planning.
clinical_pearls: STOP metformin when eGFR <30 (risk of lactic acidosis). STOP NSAIDs in all CKD patients (reduce renal blood flow, worsen function)
Anemia of CKD is primarily from decreased EPO production. Iron deficiency must be corrected BEFORE starting ESA therapy
CKD-MBD: Phosphorus binders (calcium acetate) + vitamin D replacement (calcitriol) to control PTH and prevent renal osteodystrophy

cmp.comment: Creatinine 4.2 (eGFR 16) = Stage 4 CKD. K+ 5.6 needs urgent management. HCO3 18 = metabolic acidosis. Ca 8.2, P 5.8 = CKD-MBD

cbc.comment: Hb 9.2 = anemia of CKD. Iron studies show functional iron deficiency. Will need ESA ± iron supplementation

urinalysis.comment: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR 4.2) confirms diabetic nephropathy. No hematuria/casts argues against glomerulonephritis

renal_us.comment: Small echogenic kidneys with cortical thinning = chronic irreversible disease. Rules out obstruction

hba1c.comment: HbA1c 9.2% = poorly controlled diabetes. Target <7% to slow CKD progression

missing_tests: Hepatitis B/C, HIV - Required for dialysis planning. Serum immunofixation if suspecting multiple myeloma
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, this is Stage 4 CKD approaching ESRD. Immediate: STOP metformin and ibuprofen. Start sodium bicarbonate 650mg TID for acidosis. Kayexalate or Lokelma for K+ 5.6. Start calcium acetate 667mg with meals for phosphorus. Calcitriol 0.25mcg daily for vitamin D deficiency and PTH suppression. Iron sucrose IV + Epogen for anemia when iron replete. ACEi (Lisinopril) continue for proteinuria reduction. Strict BP <130/80. Low potassium diet, fluid restriction 1.5L/day. Urgent nephrology referral for dialysis access planning (AV fistula). Discuss renal replacement options (hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, transplant).
